So, one of the two main reasons we study principles of persuasion is that persuading properly can make a massive difference in changing people's behavior, as is evident from the Texaco case. The second reason is that persuasion doesn't require the use of force, threats, or hierarchical control. Think about how you would feel if you were forced into doing something you really didn't want to do. When people feel coerced, they often find ways to undermine the goals of those doing the pressuring. If employees feel that they're frequently coerced, they may seek to leave the organization or retaliate in other ways. This is particularly problematic with high-performing employees who usually have options outside of the organization. It might be convenient for leaders to strongarm people into compliance, but they'll eventually lose talent and opportunities. Instead, leaders can get hoped for change through the ethical use of persuasion. Wise managers spend upwards of 80% of their time in verbal communication, most of it attempting to guide and persuade fellow employees.
